data.title: A Second Inflation Wave May Be Coming And Could Shock The Markets
data.description:
Inflation expectations are now starting to become unmoored. It seems hard to argue that when a 5-year CPI swap breaks out of a 2-year base, it isn't a problem. It will have significant consequences as well, because it will ultimately mean that interest rates are likely to rise rather significantly.

Given how tight credit spreads are and how high stock market valuations are, the market is not prepared for what the CPI swap market is predicting.

CPI Swaps Breakout

It's hard to imagine that a breakout in inflation expectations won't have an impact on interest rates, either, because 10- (US10Y) and 30-(US30Y) year Treasuries have traded in lockstep with CPI swaps for a long time, so the effects are likely to be felt across the market.

The cause is likely related to tariffs, rising commodity prices, and possibly increasing shipping rates. The Baltic Dry Index surged above 2,000 for the first time since the fall of 2020. With rising commodity prices, such as copper (HG1:COM) and, more importantly, iron ore (SCO:COM) (which appears poised to break out), the Baltic Dry Index is likely to continue increasing. Historically, copper and iron ore prices tend to lead the Baltic Dry Index.

All of this is likely a contributing factor, but what is undeniable is that the 5-yr CPI Swap is now trading at its highest level since the spring of 2023. It shouldn't be surprising, either, since CPI swaps tend to focus on copper over the long term. So with copper breaking out to a new all-time high following the announcement of a 50% tariff, CPI swaps are following along.

If CPI swaps continue to climb, it will likely only add to the recent pressure being seen on the back of the yield curve. Treasury rates, such as the 10-year, tend to trade in line with CPI swap pricing. The recent surge in CPI swap pricing suggests that the 10-year rate should be heading higher, and potentially considerably higher, perhaps not only to their highs in October 2023 but possibly beyond.

It is a problem, because 1-year CPI swaps are trading at higher levels than the 5-year swaps, and one needs to go back to July 2022 to find a higher pricing. Meanwhile, 2-year CPI swaps are also breaking out of that same trading range.

The Market's Warning

While one can argue about the best way to measure inflation expectations, it seems that swap pricing could be one of the better options, as a transaction is taking place. When examining the inflation market, it suggests that after two years of consolidation, inflation has not only bottomed but is also heading higher.

This isn't really a matter of opinion; it's what the market is screaming. The market can be wrong, and it has the right to change its mind; however, the risk here is that inflation resurfaces, which means that long-end rates will have to climb much further.

The market, overall, from rates to stocks and credit, is not prepared for a second inflation spike. Valuations are too, and spreads are too tight, the entire market is priced for perfection, and we all know we live in a far-from-perfect world.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Economic Conditions Are Not That Bad
data.description:
The Friday edition of the Wall Street Journal starts out this way:

"A strong start to earnings season is helping offset investors' worries over tariffs and threats to the Federal Reserve’s independence."

"Stock futures edged upward after the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite both notched record highs Thursday. (There have been six new historical highs reached by these measures over the past two weeks.) Financial updates from banks and consumer-focused companies this week have pointed to a resilient U.S. economy. Netflix lifted its annual forecasts late Thursday as it continues to see membership growth."

And, President Trump wants to fire Fed Chairman Jerome Powell?

Since the middle of April this year, the S&P 500 Stock Index has posted the following trail.

Yes, there is plenty of uncertainty floating around. Yes, people are not happy with all the tariff negotiations. Yes, people don't like many of the personal appointments and personal decisions coming out of the White House.

But....

The general picture of the U.S. economy is not bad. In fact, some dare to say that the general picture of the U.S. economy is alright.

Go figure.

And, the Federal Reserve continues its program of quantitative tightening.

Here is what the Fed has done to its portfolio of securities held outright since the most recent change...on September 18, 2024...in the severity of the reduction program.

The reduction in the portfolio of securities held outright seems to follow right along with the Fed's intention to keep shrinking the size of this portfolio.

Let me just say that the reductions taking place right now are nowhere near the magnitude of the reduction taken in the earlier part of the quantitative tightening effort.

But the Fed has continued this process, and we see it now in its 36th month.

Wow!

I don't remember ANY Federal Reserve policy stance lasting for such a long period of time.

And, the results?

The results have been phenomenal. Rising stock prices. Adequate economic growth. Falling inflation. Unemployment remaining relatively steady. Dollar value is maintained in foreign exchange markets.

In fact, looking at the economy and looking at the role the Federal Reserve has played in the results cited, more and more people are arguing that, as far as the Federal Reserve is concerned, as far as the economy is concerned, and as far as the overall picture for what is going on in the economy, the president should play his TACO card. TACO equals Trump Always Chickens Out!

In other words, Trump needs to back off his criticism of Chairman Powell and, even if he does not give Mr. Powell credit for what has been accomplished, move to a more accepting position regarding where the economy is.

I know that this might be difficult for the president to do, but in this case, he would be acting in a way that would be the most helpful to him and his efforts to Make America Great Again.

Trump would still like the Federal Reserve to lower its policy rate of interest. Although at times, Federal Reserve officials have talked about lowering the Fed's policy rate of interest, no one really seems to be taking an active role in seeking a reduction in the rate.

Here is what the rate situation looks like since September 18, 20024.

The failure to reduce the policy rate of interest is not because the banking system does not have ample reserve balances on hand.

In fact, the current commercial banking system has a massive amount of "excess" reserves on hand that they could loan out and generate some real inflationary pressure.

Take a look.

The commercial banks have roughly $3.375 trillion in "excess reserves" on their balance sheets.

This is a massive amount.

The argument could be made that the Federal Reserve could lower its policy rate of interest. There certainly is sufficient "liquidity" around in the commercial banking system to support a lower rate of interest.

But, Federal Reserve officials continue to argue that in terms of where they want to see the banking system go, it is not quite time for the policy rate to be dropped.

We are close, however. The policymakers could lower the policy rate of interest, but at present, they are just not quite ready to open the door and see interest rates decline.

To me, it seems that the Fed is concerned about what a drop in the Fed's policy rate of interest would generate in the open market.

Federal Reserve officials remain very concerned about moving too fast and opening the door to greater rates of inflation if they do.

The Federal Reserve has worked this hard to get inflation down to where it is. I believe that the concern is that Fed officials are afraid that moving too soon to lower rates will reignite inflation and all the hard work that has been done...will have to be repeated.

The call within the Federal Reserve is to err on the side of too much caution.

The rate of inflation has been falling. The rate of inflation is relatively low. Don't move too fast. Don't make people go through this painful process again.

Yet, the politicians want the suffering to end. They want to begin stimulating again in order to turn people around, make them happier with lower interest rates, and with more economic activity. And, the politicians want to get re-elected.

The battle will go on.

Whatever, economic conditions are not too bad. The stock market keeps hitting new historical highs.

And, the Federal Reserve continues its efforts to remove all the "excess" monies it injected into the banking system during the CovidCOVID-19emic and the subsequent period when the economy went into recession.

And, President Trump pushes for reducing interest rates.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Crypto Clarity, Financial Earnings, JNJ And JOBY
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Crypto legislation back on track, Bitcoin hitting highs (0:20). Financial earnings holding ground (2:00). JNJ beats expectations, absorbing tariff hits (3:25). Tesla report coming next week; news priced in (4:15). Consumer earnings coming, (5:50). Joby Aviation back on investors' radar (9:15).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Welcome back to another week of Wall Street Roundup. Thanks for joining us, Brian.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here as usual.

RS: So lead us into the weekend. Talk to us about what we've seen this week.What's been top of mind for you?

BS: This week, there's been a lot of wrangling in Washington. There were some hurdles with the crypto regulation legislation that's been making its way through Congress, but that seems back on track now.

You see Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has been higher lately though. It's had trouble getting above the $120,000 mark. So I would keep that in mind as a spot.

But if you look at the shovels and pick axe level of Bitcoin related stocks, you see Coinbase (COIN) is up 5% in the last five days. It's setting a new fifty two week high. It's up 67% year to date.

Meanwhile, Circle (CRCL) is up 23% over the past five days. So this is during the period of time when the politicians were ironing out the legislation, Circle's off its post IPO highs, which it reached in June. But like I said, still up strongly during the week.

So I think you see some upside in the overall crypto space just as there gets to be some clarity about the regulatory environment.

After Trump was elected, there was a spike in Bitcoin and crypto stocks, with the belief that there would be a supportive regulatory environment, and you're starting to see that actually come to pass. I think you're just fulfilling some of the promise that was made a few months ago going into this administration.

RS: And earning season is well underway. Yesterday, you were talking about the airlines. What would you say from the financial sector?

BS: Financials were the main theme of earnings this week. We're still ramping up in terms of earning season, so there's not a lot of huge names outside of financials.

It was a relatively unimpressed market. It was basically a shrug following the financial earnings. So you see most of the major players up or down in a range of plus or minus 2% following their results.

One standout is Citi (C), which is up about 5% in the the last five days, but overall, a flat performance. If you look at the (XLF), which is the sector ETF for financials, it's basically flat over the last five days.

So the market took those earnings in stride. They're in line with what people expected. And you have to remember that financials were up going into it. So names like Citi were up 29% year to date. Goldman Sachs (GS) was up 24%. The overall XLF was up 8% year to date.

I think that as a kickoff to the earning season, I would take that as a win that the already strong performing financial sector held its ground after its earnings. So that part of it seems like it's well within expectations.

Otherwise, you saw J&J (JNJ) rose about 6% after its earnings. At one point, it hit the biggest intraday gain in nearly two years. Following that, it beat expectations, on both earnings and revenue, raised its forecasts. It cited forks tailwinds and strong operational performances for its earnings.

So far, it's been able to absorb the tariff hit. Obviously, that's something to keep an eye on for big multinational companies like JNJ, whether or not the tariffs that are currently coming online are going to have an impact.

So far so good on that front, at least using JNJ as a a bellwether for that, but I think we'll see as the earning season heats up how concerned companies are about that.

RS: Anything else to add to the earnings conversation or what investors could look for next week?

BS: So next week, the big name is going to be Tesla (TSLA). As always, it's going to be a tension between results and the future prospects.

Investors are already aware that the company has taken a hit recently, both the brand hit that it took with Elon Musk, DOGE, and then the feud with Trump, the whole drama there obviously put Tesla in the spotlight in a way that wasn't exactly beneficial to its brands.

That news is largely priced into the stock. At this point I think investors, like a bullish investor, would be betting that it bottomed out in Q1 and Q2 is gonna show signs of recovery there. So I think avoiding a disaster on that front would be step one for a Tesla bull.

And then I think a lot of the discussion around the stock is going to focus on its more innovative products. Humanoid robots, robotaxis, things like that. Look out for commentary for that. In a lot of ways, that's where Musk excels as a businessman is the showmanship aspect of it.

The earnings conference call and the presentation there is probably gonna give him a chance to reset expectations and kind of get back into consciousness, at least on Wall Street, as a businessman and less as a political player.

RS: Anything else to add to the conversation to look forward to for next week, either macro or other stock specific news happening?

BS: Yeah. Just to look at a few themes that'll likely come up. There's some consumer players that are set to report earnings. Coca Cola (KO) is coming out, Southwest (LUV), is coming out in the airline space.

You have AutoNation (AN), an auto dealer. That might be an interesting look at whether or not consumers are putting off purchases of large items due to the current pricing environments.

Meanwhile, on Friday, you have (HCA), which is a hospital operator coming out with the commentary coming out of the big beautiful bill about the situation for hospitals, rural hospitals specifically, but hospitals in general. I think that earnings call will be interesting in that industry just to see where industry leaders are looking for the future in the new regulatory and federal spending environment.

RS: Coke (KO) was in the news this week, hit some headlines with the president. Anything to note there?

BS: The environment we're in, there's such an overlap between Washington and Wall Street. I think that's gonna be inevitable from here on out is there's gonna be feuds that spark up.

Disney (DIS) was the kind of political target for a while. I don't think we've seen the last of of that either. I think investors are getting used to that as part of the noise surrounding stock trading now is just where do certain big name brands stand in regard to the administration.

RS: And there's always been, right, that sort of tension at times between Wall Street and Washington. Do you feel like it's the most pronounced now or do you feel it's just the most nakedly displayed, let's say?

BS: I think the tone is different because you can think back to during the Obama administration, during the Bailout era, there was a huge overlap between Washington and Wall Street. It was more directly financial related. It was also in the wake of a major crisis, so it was predictable that the government will get involved.

Also thinking back to the Clinton administration, there was a lot of talk about antitrust in Microsoft (MSFT) and breaking up the company, perhaps things like that.

It's not certainly unheard of that there would be tension or disputes that rise up among large companies and the federal government, but the tone does seem to have changed. It's more personal now than it was. It's less regulatory and more political in nature.

So again, I feel like since the first round of tariff stock movements, in the April time frame when the market crashed on the fear that the tariffs were gonna come to effect and then the bounce back that happened when those were delayed, even now when those headlines are coming out, you don't see the huge swings now that you saw back then.

I feel like investors are becoming immune to it in a certain way. They're sort of building it into their mental models on how to invest, that this is just the tone at which these discussions are going to continue for at least the next few years.

RS: Bob and adapt. Anything else, Brian?

BS: One company that I just thought was worth noting because it's kinda interesting. Joby (JOBY). It makes electronic air taxis. It's up about 39% in the past week. It is set to double its production in its California facility. It's going to be able to make up to 24 air taxis.

This is a company that came public a few years ago in 2021 through a SPAC. It got a lot of initial excitement and then fell off dramatically when it became clear that it wasn't gonna meet ambitious timelines that it set forth.

Their goal is to have an air taxi service. The flagship that they've been talking about for a while is from lower Manhattan to JFK where they can fly you there in I think seven minutes or something like that was the time frame given.

That hasn't materialized yet, but the recent moves that the company's made upgrading its facilities, there's also been some regulatory easing. They're looking to launch their first service in Dubai. So there's signs that the company is materializing some of the promise they had and so it's been up dramatically lately.

So just as a check-in from a company that was hot a while ago and kind of fell off the radar, I think it's interesting to note that it's back on the radar.

data.title: SPLG: My Favorite S&P 500 ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPLG) is a premier choice among S&P 500 ETFs by combining low costs, long-term performance, and tax efficiencies. SPLG's low cost expense ratio is the lowest in its category, offering exceptional compounding for long-term investors. Additionally, the liquidity is very strong for retail investors, supporting tight bid/ask spreads during the purchase and sales of the fund.

Despite concerns about market overvaluation and a relatively low dividend yield, the S&P 500's tech-heavy composition supports a higher valuation from broad exposure to stocks with higher returns on capital. For investors debating whether to wait for a market dip, SPLG reinforces the principle that time in the market beats timing the market. I have initiated a Buy rating and affirm that SPLG remains my go-to choice for S&P 500 exposure.

Fund Objectives, Fees, and Liquidity

The SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF is a low-cost fund that seeks to provide investment results that correspond to the total return of the S&P 500. It is one of SPDR's core ETFs which is designed to provide broad, diversified exposure to core U.S. asset classes. The fund currently has 503 holdings in the portfolio and contains only 0.02% in cash.

But with all of the available ETFs that track the S&P 500, why should investors choose State Street's SPLG fund? Among the crowd, which includes (VOO), (IVV), and (SPY), SPLG has the lowest expense ratio at 0.02%. VOO and IVV are a close second at 0.03%, and SPY is 0.09%. This may seem like an inconsequential difference in expenses, but investors with a long-term holding period will significantly boost their returns with lower fees - even if it's just a few basis points.

I have included the table below to highlight the four major S&P 500 funds previously mentioned. The figures can be found on Seeking Alpha's site for each respective fund and are subject to change based on fund inflows/outflows, trading volume, and actual performance. The total returns and dividend yield will be discussed in the next section.

(SPLG) (VOO) (IVV) (SPY)

Expenses

 0.02% 0.03% 0.03% 0.09%
Avg. Daily Share Volume 3Mo. 9.15M 6.59M 5.91M 69.68M
Assets (AUM) $77.2B $1.49T $635.4B $645.4B
10-Year Performance 199.03% 202.71% 201.52% 201.94%
Dividend Yield 1.21% 1.21% 1.28% 1.15%

SPLG offers exceptional liquidity for investors. Over 9.15 million shares are traded daily. While this is not highest among peers (SPY trades 54.6 million shares per day), it is more than enough liquidity for a retail investor looking to make buy/sell orders at tight big/ask spreads.

The liquidity ensures investors have the opportunity to make purchases or sales essentially at SPLG's net asset value during all market cycles. The premium/discount is usually within 1% of the fund's net asset value, which is especially critical for individual investors who make routine monthly or quarterly portfolio rebalancing decisions.

Performance, Dividends and Tax Considerations

Among the same peers previously mentioned, SPLG offers the highest five-year return at more than 95% - an annualized return of over 14%. Investors have captured ~0.50% higher returns than the three peers while maintaining the lowest expense ratio. It is important to note, however, that on a 10-year annualized basis SPLG has the lowest return at 199.03%. Ultimately, the return variability among peers is low and consistently within +/- 3% over a decade.

SPLG boasts a current quant dividend grade of A. The dividend yield is currently 1.21% and has a strong 10-year dividend compound annual growth rate of 6.97%, providing crucial dividend growth for income-seeking investors.

The quant score understandably gives the 1.21% yield a D rating but gives the consecutive years of dividend growth an A+. So, while the entry yield may be low, investors can expect their dividends to reliably grow over time.

Dividends, which SPLG distributes quarterly, comes with a tax bill that investors must consider. The quarterly distributions are advantageously taxed as qualified dividends, which carry a tax rate of 0%, 15%, or 20%, depending on the investor's total income. In order to meet the qualified dividends criteria, investors must hold SLPG for more than 60 days during the 121-day period surrounding the ex-dividend date.

Unlike some funds, capital gains on SPLG are tax-efficient due to its in-kind redemption process. Investors who hold the ETF for more than one year will pay the same tax rate as the qualified dividend rate mentioned previously while a holding period of less than one year will result in gains taxed as ordinary income.

Value Consideration and Timing the Market

Many investors fear the S&P 500 is overvalued. The index is now back in striking distance of its all-time high of $6,302.04, and P/E valuations appear stretched. The prevailing question is constantly asked: "Why buy the S&P index now when I can wait for the market to drop and buy in then?". There are two important reasons investors should avoid this thinking.

First, the S&P 500 valuation of 25x P/E may not be as high as investors think on a relative basis. Per chapter two of Tim Koller's "Measuring and Managing the Value of Companies," the current tech-dominant market contains more companies with higher rates of return on invested capital, justifying a higher valuation (Koller, Tim, Marc Goedhart, David Wessels, and McKinsey & Company Inc. Valuation: Measuring and Managing the Value of Companies. 8th ed., Wiley, 2025.).

I created the table below to highlight the comparison of reinvestment opportunities and its effect on valuation.

Company ROIC P/E Ratio Justification
A 20% 30x High efficiency, strong reinvestment potential
B 12% 16x Low efficiency, limited growth prospects

Company A has a higher return on invested capital, meaning it can reinvest profits at higher returns. A higher ROIC signals strong future earnings growth, which supports a higher P/E ratio. The tech-dominant S&P 500 has more companies today with higher returns on invested capital, supporting a higher valuation.

The second reason to consider investing in the S&P 500 follows the adage "time in the market beats timing the market". Trying to time the market means guessing when to buy low and sell high. But history shows that staying invested in the market consistently outperforms jumping in and out. This is echoed by investing legend Warren Buffett and asset manager Vanguard.

Risks

SPLG contains two main risks that could make the ETF less suitable for certain investors. First, because of the high concentration of tech stocks in the index, SPLG could have heightened volatility which may be unsuitable for investors that have near-term cash needs.

For example, the 2022 drawdown saw the SPLG drop from a high of ~$56 to a low of ~$42, a 25% price drop in ten months. While long-term investors may ignore this volatility, shorter-term investors may want to consider ETFs with lower volatility, like Vanguard's U.S. Minimum Volatility ETF (VFMV).

The second main risk relates to income-seeking investors. SPLG's dividend yield of 1.21% is low relative to other ETFs available in the market. The S&P 500's dividend yield has declined from around 4% in the 1908's to ~1.20% as of 2025. This is primarily driven by more companies in the S&P 500 choosing to use free cash flow for share repurchases over dividends. Income-seeking investors may be more interested in Vanguard's High-Yield S&P 500 ETF (VYM) in this scenario.

Conclusion

SPLG is a best-in-class S&P 500 ETF. The fund is a low-cost leader who provides investors with broad exposure to the S&P 500 index. Its in-kind redemption process supports tax efficiencies for qualified dividends and capital gains, ensuring investors optimize their tax situation.

Despite broad fears of market overvaluation, now is a great time for investors to add SPLG to their portfolio. Remember, time in the market beats timing the market.

Although I have initiated a Buy rating, all investors have different situations and those averse to market volatility or income-seeking investors may want to consider different investment opportunities.

data.title: Alphabet Is Not A Victim And Seems Ready To Fight
data.description:
In May, I wrote about Alphabet Inc. aka Google (GOOG, GOOGL) and this article will be a supplement to that article. Therefore, I will not analyze the financial issues here and what I have already analyzed in the last article. And also, this article will be part of my latest series of articles, where I tried to analyze the general situation in the AI market, starting with an introductory article, which you can read at this link.

Here I will analyze, so to speak, the general current position of Alphabet in the competitive war that has begun between tech giants.

Victim in defense

Google currently looks like a victim of aggression, suffering losses, which is why its shares look bad. The market narrative that has emerged to date paints a picture where Alphabet acts as a lumbering giant, caught off guard by the attack of Microsoft's (MSFT) alliance with OpenAI. Many accuse the company of bureaucracy, delayed reaction and defensive strategy. It seems that the tech giant, which itself stood at the origins of the AI revolution, has become its main victim.

But I believe that this view is just a tactic and it misses the fundamental advantages that only Google has and which, ultimately, may become decisive in the race for the development of AI technology. But let's take everything in order.

Alphabet's current problems are constant antitrust proceedings around the world, these are huge expenses on data centers, the effectiveness of which investors question. But the main problem is the attack on the company's sales markets by competitors.

The alliance of Microsoft and OpenAI has targeted the very heart and core of the company's business. The news that Apple (AAPL) is implementing ChatGPT has created a threat that Google may lose a huge market for search queries in iOS and will lose its dominant position in this market, along the way losing a significant share of revenue. In addition, OpenAI recently announced its intention to create its own AI browser with the support of Microsoft. And this is already the second blow to the company, because in addition to the audience using iOS, the invaders want to attack the position of the Chrome browser.

If you look at the situation only through the prism of these threats, the picture for Google really looks alarming.

Google's Advantages

However, while the market is obsessed with these tactical battles, it does not see, or does not want to see, the strengths that the company has and which give very strong advantages. The main asset of Alphabet, unlike its competitors, is a huge data moat. And also the fact that Alphabet is the most vertically integrated and self-sufficient company.

Unlike Microsoft, which depends on OpenAI, or Apple, which uses other technologies, Google has full vertical integration. The company has its own artificial intelligence Gemini, huge financial resources, a huge customer base, a huge data center infrastructure, a development strategy based on its own strengths and resources. And a sea of ​​information accumulated over the years. Through its search engine, Google knows what people want to buy, where to go, what worries them and what they dream about, both in real time and in the past. Thanks to YouTube, you can see what videos people watch, what music is popular, what hobbies they enjoy in different countries. This is a gigantic array of visual and audio data. Thanks to maps, Google knows where people are and what places they visit. And don't forget about the mail service.

This ocean of continuous, multimodal data that no one else has yet. And all this can be integrated with Gemini AI. Google's Artificial Intelligence has the greatest access to information and, accordingly, the greatest opportunities for self-improvement and development.

And as I believe, it was precisely this state that was the reason that Alphabet did not play at competition for a long time, did not build schemes, plans of attack to capture other people's markets. But they were simply engaged in creation, relying on the development of AI technology itself. And I must say that they succeeded in this. At the moment, it is no longer possible to say for sure who is better - Gemini or ChatGPT. Although in 2023, ChatGPT was the leader.

Although Alphabet looked like a giant that is sitting on the defensive and whose markets are being taken away, it did not sit idle, but relied on moving forward and developing the technology itself, and not on the redistribution of old markets.

And in this context, if we consider that everything in the world is relative, then OpenAI and Microsoft went on the attack to capture Alphabet markets, but lost their main trump card - the unconditional leadership of ChatGPT. On the other hand, Google allowed itself to be beaten, but caught up with OpenAI in the main issue - Gemini caught up, and may have already overtaken ChatGPT.

Google is ready to fight

But the attacks of competitors could not go unnoticed by the company's management. And judging by the latest news, Alphabet will not sit on the defensive any longer, but will also attack competitors.

The recent news that Google aggressively intercepted the team of the AI startup Windsurf (a direct competitor of GitHub Copilot) right from under the noses of OpenAI and Microsoft is a clear signal. The empire strikes back.

This is not just a talent acquisition. This is a targeted attack on Microsoft's most successful AI product. In my article about Microsoft, I wrote that they have an excellent defensive position. So if Copilot is the powerful armor of the Tiger tank, then Google has just acquired a "120-millimeter cannon" capable of penetrating this armor.

With the Windsurf team in its pocket, it is possible to launch an attack on Microsoft products. And what's more, for Alphabet, this deal only makes sense in the context of competition with Copilot and competition already on Microsoft's territory.

This move proves that Google will not passively watch attacks on its business. It is ready for asymmetric, aggressive counterattacks and the war of tech giants is no longer one-sided.

The Best Product Wins - Google's Final Bet

The chess moves of management, attacks by companies on each other, the redistribution of old markets are, of course, important, but as I wrote in one of the articles, the winner in this story will not be the one who comes up with the best business scheme, but the one who creates the best product, the one whose AI is better and the final choice will be up to the consumer.

Remember Apple under Steve Jobs - their triumph was ensured not by behind-the-scenes deals, but by the appearance of the iPhone - a product that was an order of magnitude better than anything that existed on the market. Financial success was only a consequence of this technological breakthrough.

And I think that investors are missing the most important thing, which is that Google is focused on improving the AI technology itself and is completely self-sufficient in this matter, unlike other companies that are forced to enter into alliances and are dependent on each other.

As I wrote in my article on Microsoft, their partner OpenAI can potentially enter into a partnership with Apple. That is, their agreement with the implementation of ChatGPT in iOS can develop further. And then Microsoft and Apple can become competitors for influence over OpenAI and these are their risks. Alphabet does not have such risks!

As for antitrust issues, attacks on Google can deprive this company of a monopoly, and due to this, eliminate the problem with the monopoly.

Summary

The market today behaves asymmetrically. It rewards Microsoft with a high P/E ratio for their aggressive strategy. And it punishes Google with a low P/E for the fact that they are in the role of the attacked party. At the time of writing my May article, Google's TTM P/E was at 20, which is significantly lower than their historical average of 30. And Microsoft's TTM P/E was at 39. The difference between the companies is almost 2 times!!!

But now that Google has shown that it is also ready to attack, this asymmetry in valuations loses all meaning. If this is a battle between two equally valuable giants that strike at each other's vulnerabilities, then why should one of them be worth much less than the other in multiples? Yes, Microsoft struck first and received bonuses for this, and Google suffered losses. But now they will beat each other, and both companies will suffer losses and receive bonuses from tactical victories. This means that their risks are approximately equal.

But as I see it, the market has not yet digested this fact. It still evaluates Google as a passive victim, and not as an active fighter ready to strike back.

In view of the above, I believe that the current pessimism and investor focus on short-term tactical risks create a unique opportunity to buy shares of a company that has the strongest and deepest fundamental advantages in the AI race at an unreasonably low valuation.

My recommendation for Alphabet, Inc. is a Strong Buy.

data.title: Simmons First National Corporation 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Simmons First National Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Rigetti Computing: Irrational Exuberance Has Returned Again
data.description:
Rigetti Computing: Irrational Exuberance Has Returned Once More

The stock of Rigetti Computing, Inc. (NASDAQ:RGTI) has gained more than 75% from my previous Sell rating? And more than 1,300% over the past year, as early investors who hopped on board benefited markedly from the incredible rise to the top. That's a crazy rally over that period of time, isn't it? Bullish investors might contend that I don't get it. After all, RGTI recently announced that it has set a new performance milestone that also got investors all ebullient this week, seeing the stock advancing further from last week.

Yet, with RGTI continuing to hover under the $21 level that marked the stock's peak in late 2024, should we expect another renaissance that bullish quantum computing investors will likely point you to?

Accordingly, Rigetti's quantum system has attained the 99.5% median two qubit gate fidelity on its 36-qubit architecture. Hence, I believe the market elevated its optimism on RGTI's capabilities on scaling up rapidly on its error rate reduction, inching several steps closer to potentially attaining quantum advantage. Would the achievements position RGTI credibly toward its year end goal of delivering 99.5% fidelity on its next-gen 100+ qubits system? I think we will get to hear more from management when Rigetti reports its Q2 earnings scorecard on August 7.

Nevertheless, forward-looking investors aren't biding their time to add more exposure, as they sought to wreak havoc and inflict more pain on the investor psychology of Rigetti short sellers (20% short interest ratio).

RGTI: Solid Roadmap, But Quantum Advantage Yet Isn't Clear Yet

As one of the proponents of the superconducting architecture, Rigetti faces intense competition against big tech rivals. In particular, IBM (IBM) is striving to develop and build a "fault tolerant" quantum computer by 2029, potentially a world-first watershed moment for the industry.

Notwithstanding the immense challenges against its more well-endowed rivals with profitable businesses, RGTI has also demonstrated a credible roadmap over the past ten years (seen above), underpinning the conviction of its investors in the company's ability to keep pace/surpass more prominent superconducting peers.

And, I must highlight that since my previous writeup in March, the quantum world has seen a paradigm shift in conviction from Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang (NVDA) as compared to the earlier year's skepticism. As unveiled at NVDA's June GTC in Paris, Huang went even as far to underscore that quantum tech has reached an "inflection point," lending credence to the long-term bullish narrative for the industry.

RGTI: Balance Sheet More Secure, But Enough To Attain Quantum Advantage?

Yet, we must also be cognizant that deeper penetration or commercialization of the tech might not be ready until after 2030. That seems to have support from McKinsey's even more sanguine report in June, highlighting that the industry. The consulting firm promulgated its conviction that 2025 should be remembered as the year when quantum computing was transformed from "concept to reality." Investors who partook in the early innings of this plausible hockey stick inflection could see the market opportunity surge from just $4B in 2024 to over $72B by 2035.

Yet, we must also be judicious that unless we see real world quantum advantage being validated (but current quantum computers remain "too noisy"), such projections are mired in substantive uncertainties that should make us wary about according too high a valuation multiple to even leading players like Rigetti.

To the credit of Rigetti, the firm capitalized on its highly pricey valuation multiples to raise $350M in a recent ATM offering in June. That's an astute maneuver, as it allowed the company to more than double its cash and equivalents buffer to $575M.

Based on Rigetti's 10K report, the company projected that it had enough cash runway to sustain its operation for at "least the next three years." Note that the company's accumulative cash burn from FY2025-27 is anticipated to tally to about $184M. Coupled with a debt free balance sheet, it seems to have afforded a clearer path for RGTI to invest further and possibly gain a wider edge over its rivals in the next five years as the technology moves forward toward commercialization.

Buy Or Sell RGTI Stock?

So, should we then all rush in and attempt to compensate for missing out on buying RGTI's plunge that bottomed out in April. Note that the robust recovery over the past three months hasn't been stymied after a relatively subdued month in June (RGTI then finished below its May highs).

With RGTI's current price action potentially breaking above its May's peak, it does seem like we could move toward a feasible re-test of its 2024/2025 zenith. So, should I still keep to my bearish posture on the stock, notwithstanding the recent gains?

Yes, I will. While the quantum computing evangelists want us to help support the stock after last year's (already) massive surge, the rapid developments in the field suggest it's not reasonable to determine which firm is on a clearer path to gain quantum advantage. Yet, with RGTI being valued at a forward EV/Revenue multiple of 450x, have we simply taken the enthusiasm to bubbly heights once again?

And note that there's no guarantee that RGTI buyers can stage a decisive breakout above the $21.5 level, that might also see more sellers returning to defend the previous selling resistance zone.

No matter how bullish we are, we need to prudently question whether irrational exuberance has reared its ugly head again before over-optimistic buyers could face another blow-off top later?

Rating: Maintain Sell.
